sections.title: A Tour Around My Village
sections.content_summary: The chapter opens with Chandan, a village boy, introducing his community — a playground, a post office, a well, and a banyan tree where elders gather. Students observe a detailed village scene and identify how various people and places (shopkeeper, road, health centre, police, school) are interconnected. The section establishes that communities are built on shared spaces and mutual dependence.

tasks.task_text: Look at the picture of Chandan's village. What do you see in the picture? Describe below.
Explore your locality; observe the people and places around you. Write your observations about how the people and places you see are connected to each other in the table given below.

sections.title: Van Mahotsav: A Festival for Planting Trees
sections.content_summary: Chandan and his school friends prepare for Van Mahotsav — an annual Indian tree-planting festival held from 1 July to 7 July during the rainy season. Students discuss why shared public places like parks, schools, hospitals and bus stops are important, what would change if these disappeared, and how to keep them clean. The section introduces community care of natural surroundings as a civic responsibility.

tasks.task_text: Why do you think it is important to have places like parks, schools, hospitals and bus stops in your locality?
How would life be different if one or more of these places in your locality did not exist?
What can you do to keep these places clean and safe?
Let us create a colourful poster to help Chandan and his friends invite people from their neighbourhood to participate in the Van Mahotsav.

sections.title: Helping Hands in the Community
sections.content_summary: Villagers — parents and community members — gather under the banyan tree to plan the Van Mahotsav celebrations. Each person contributes a different skill: a carpenter fixes benches, an electrician repairs the street light, and a plant nursery owner (Noor) brings saplings. Students identify community helpers and their occupations, and explore the tools, materials, and skills different workers use through a structured table activity including a mason as an example.

tasks.task_text: Identify the people who help in maintaining your locality. What are their occupations?
Look at the table below. List different kinds of work required for developing and maintaining the park. Also, mention the material, tools and skills required. An example is given here.

Find out from your family members or neighbours about their work. What questions would you ask them? Here are some examples: (a) What do you like the most about your work? (b) What types of tools or things do you use? (c) What challenges do you face?

sections.title: Celebrating Van Mahotsav
sections.content_summary: The Van Mahotsav celebration unfolds with families and children working in organised groups for cleaning, planting saplings, watering, and cooking. The section also presents a real-life example from Kanker, Chhattisgarh (2024), where the entire community built a bamboo-and-stone bridge over the overflowing Chinar river in just two days, enabling students and villagers to cross safely. Students discuss the value of community solidarity in times of need.

tasks.task_text: It is important for communities to come together in times of need. What do you think about this statement?
Do people in your locality come together and support each other? Can you remember one such occasion?

sections.title: Sharing a Meal
sections.content_summary: After the tree plantation, the community shares a collective feast — vegetables and cereals contributed by villagers, served on banana leaves. After eating, the leaves are disposed of in a pit to make manure, improving soil quality. The section introduces IKS cultural practices: Uruka (first day of Magh Bihu, 14 January) in Assam, where communities build a bamboo-and-hay hut called Bhela Ghar and celebrate with a communal feast. Students reflect on how families and communities work together during festivals.

tasks.task_text: Think about your family or community. How do you work together to help each other while preparing for a festival or any other occasion?

sections.title: Let us reflect
sections.content_summary: Students consolidate learning through the 'Our School, Our Team!' reflection. They map the roles of different people in their school community (teachers, students, caretakers, bus drivers), conduct a group activity (tree plantation, cleanliness drive, or nature artefact-making), solve community-place riddles, and learn about the Khetala practice from Sikkim — agricultural specialists who come together at harvest time to help one another, exemplifying community bonds. Students also explore a local traditional skill or craft from their own family.

tasks.task_text: What are some of the ways by which we can take care of nature and our neighbourhood? List a few.
How does helping others and working together make our community better?
Write the different role people play in your school — teachers, students, caretakers, bus drivers, etc.

Form small groups of 4–5 students. Choose one fun activity to perform from the list below: (a) Tree plantation (b) Cleanliness drive (c) Making artefacts with materials from nature (something useful like a leaf-plate or a decorative item like a garland).
Match the riddles with their solutions hidden in the pictures.
Choose a public place of your interest, and create a similar riddle about it for your friends to solve.

Create three groups in the class. Each group would think about a situation in their locality that needs to be improved. Identify activities that can make a difference to the situation. This could be related to cleaning, helping others, organising an event, etc. Complete the table below.
Ask your family about any one special skill or craft (for example— basket making, wall painting, pottery) that is famous in your region.

dual_strand.natural: Shared natural spaces (parks, trees, environment) and community dependence on nature
dual_strand.human_cultural: Van Mahotsav tree-planting festival; community cooperation and civic responsibility; IKS practices — Uruka/Magh Bihu feast with Bhela Ghar (Assam), Khetala harvest cooperation (Sikkim), banana-leaf composting
